content.text: What it is

Error budget burn alerts are a class of SLO-based alert that fires when a service is consuming its error budget at a rate fast enough to exhaust it before the measurement window closes. Rather than alerting on instantaneous error rates — which produces false positives from transient spikes — burn rate alerts measure the ratio of current error consumption to the steady-state rate that would exactly exhaust the budget at window end. A burn rate of 1x means the service is on a trajectory to use exactly 100% of its budget; a burn rate of 14x means it will exhaust the full monthly budget in about two days.

This concept, popularized by Google's Site Reliability Engineering practice, is especially valuable for agentic AI services because LLM inference failures tend to cluster (a model endpoint goes down, every in-flight request fails) rather than arriving uniformly. A burn-rate alert detects this clustering pattern quickly without requiring the engineering team to hand-tune per-service error rate thresholds.
Why it matters

Traditional threshold alerts on instantaneous error rates suffer from two failure modes: they fire too often during short spikes that do not actually threaten the SLO, and they fail to fire during slow-burn degradations that steadily erode the budget over days without ever crossing a high per-minute threshold. Both failure modes damage on-call quality — the first through alert fatigue, the second through missed incidents that cause SLO breaches.

For agentic AI workloads, this matters doubly. Agent tasks often have long durations; a 10-minute task that fails at minute 9 represents a far larger customer impact than a 100ms API call failure, yet both register as a single error event. Error budget burn rate alerts surface the cumulative impact of these failures relative to the service's reliability commitment, giving the on-call engineer a signal that is proportional to actual SLO risk rather than raw event count.
Benefits
Dramatically fewer false-positive pages compared to raw error rate thresholds, reducing alert fatigue without sacrificing detection.
Proportional urgency: the burn rate directly translates into remaining time before SLO breach, giving on-call engineers a clear sense of how quickly they must respond.
Catches slow degradations: a burn rate of 2x sustained over several days triggers a low-urgency alert even though no single minute crosses a high error rate threshold.
Works with any SLI: the burn rate formula applies equally to availability, latency, task completion rate, and quality SLIs — unifying the alerting model across heterogeneous signal types.
Incentivizes reliability investment: because the budget is finite and shared across the month, teams feel the cost of each incident as depleted budget rather than as an isolated event.
Trade-offs and limitations

Detection lag: the multi-window approach requires the burn to be sustained over minutes or hours before a page fires, meaning a high-severity outage that resolves in under 5 minutes may not trigger a page even if it consumed significant budget.

Complex configuration: setting multi-window burn rate thresholds requires understanding the relationship between burn rate, window length, and budget consumption, which is less intuitive than a simple error rate percentage.
SLI quality dependency: burn rate alerts are only as useful as the underlying SLI. A poorly instrumented SLI that undercounts errors will produce misleadingly low burn rates.

Early-month / late-month asymmetry: a given burn rate implies a shorter time to budget exhaustion later in the month when less budget remains — alert sensitivity is inherently time-varying within the measurement window.
Low-traffic services: burn rate calculations become unreliable when request volumes are very low, as a single failure can appear as an extremely high burn rate due to statistical noise.
How it works

The core formula is straightforward. Define the error budget rate as the fraction of events that are allowed to fail while meeting the SLO. For a 99.9% availability SLO, the error budget rate is 0.001 (one in a thousand requests may fail). The burn rate is then:

A burn rate of 1 means the service is using the budget at exactly the steady-state rate. A burn rate of 14 means the budget will be exhausted in approximately 1/14 of the window length — about 2 days for a 30-day window, or about 1.7 hours for a 24-hour window.

The key insight behind multi-window alerting is that a single short window produces too many false positives (a 5-minute spike fires the alert, then clears), while a single long window responds too slowly (a sustained outage isn't detected for hours). Pairing a short evaluation window with a long lookback window — and requiring both to exceed the threshold simultaneously — eliminates both failure modes. The short window provides detection speed; the long window provides confirmation that the burn is sustained.

The Google SRE book defines four canonical alert windows for a 30-day SLO window: (1) 14x burn rate over both 1h and 5min windows, which fires if the budget would be exhausted in 2 days; (2) 6x burn rate over 6h and 30min windows; (3) 3x burn rate over 1d and 6h windows; (4) 1x burn rate check in the monthly budget report. For shorter SLO windows (7-day or 24-hour), the absolute window lengths must be scaled down proportionally.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Error budget burn rate alert tiers and multi-window architecture

content.text: An SLO definition feeds a burn rate calculator that computes the ratio of current error rate to the budget-exhaustion rate across multiple window pairs. The result flows into tiered alert rules that route differently based on urgency — from immediate page through ticket-only — with each tier requiring both windows in the pair to exceed the threshold simultaneously.
When to use it

Burn rate alerts are the right primary alerting mechanism for any service that has a defined SLO and sufficient request volume to make percentile-based calculations statistically meaningful. They replace or supplement raw threshold alerts on error rate, latency, and availability.

They are particularly effective when the team has already experienced alert fatigue from too many low-severity pages, or when past incidents were discovered too late because slow degradations never crossed a static error rate threshold. For agentic AI services with long-running tasks, burn rate alerts based on task completion rate are more meaningful than per-request error rates because they account for the higher cost of each failed task.

Avoid burn rate alerts for very low-traffic services (fewer than a few hundred requests per evaluation window) where statistical noise dominates. In those cases, count-based alerts or absolute downtime thresholds are more reliable. Similarly, avoid burn rate alerts without established SLOs — the burn rate is meaningless without a defined target.
How to implement
Define the SLO precisely: specify the SLI (what signal), the target (e.g., 99.9%), and the measurement window (e.g., 30 days rolling).
Compute the error budget rate: for a 99.9% SLO, the error budget rate is 0.001.
Choose window pairs: for a 30-day SLO window, use (1h / 5min), (6h / 30min), and (1d / 6h) as the fast/slow pairs for tiers 1, 2, and 3 respectively.
Compute burn rate thresholds: tier 1 uses 14x because 1/14 of 30 days is ~2 days (the urgency threshold); tier 2 uses 6x (5-day exhaustion); tier 3 uses 3x (10-day exhaustion).
Write alert rules: in Prometheus, create recording rules for the burn rate on each window, then alert rules that check both windows simultaneously.
Configure routing: tier 1 pages the on-call immediately; tier 2 pages during business hours or with a 30-minute delay; tier 3 creates a Jira ticket and posts to Slack.
Write runbooks: each alert tier should link to a runbook with specific investigation steps for the service, not generic SLO advice.
Test with synthetic load: verify that injecting errors at a known rate produces the expected burn rate signal before relying on the alerts in production.
Examples
Prometheus recording rules and alert definitions for a 30-day availability SLO at 99.9%.

# prometheus/rules/slo-burn-rate.yaml
# SLO: 99.9% availability over 30 days
# Error budget rate: 0.001

groups:
  - name: slo_burn_rate_recording
    interval: 1m
    rules:
      # Short evaluation windows
      - record: slo:agent_availability:error_rate5m
        expr: |
          1 - (
            sum(rate(http_requests_total{service="agent-api",code!~"5.."}[5m]))
            /
            sum(rate(http_requests_total{service="agent-api"}[5m]))
          )

      - record: slo:agent_availability:error_rate1h
        expr: |
          1 - (
            sum(rate(http_requests_total{service="agent-api",code!~"5.."}[1h]))
            /
            sum(rate(http_requests_total{service="agent-api"}[1h]))
          )

      # Longer evaluation windows
      - record: slo:agent_availability:error_rate30m
        expr: |
          1 - (
            sum(rate(http_requests_total{service="agent-api",code!~"5.."}[30m]))
            /
            sum(rate(http_requests_total{service="agent-api"}[30m]))
          )

      - record: slo:agent_availability:error_rate6h
        expr: |
          1 - (
            sum(rate(http_requests_total{service="agent-api",code!~"5.."}[6h]))
            /
            sum(rate(http_requests_total{service="agent-api"}[6h]))
          )

  - name: slo_burn_rate_alerts
    rules:
      # Tier 1: 14x burn rate on both 5min and 1h windows
      # Exhausts 30-day budget in ~2 days
      - alert: AgentSLOBurnRateTier1
        expr: |
          slo:agent_availability:error_rate5m  > (14 * 0.001)
          and
          slo:agent_availability:error_rate1h  > (14 * 0.001)
        for: 2m
        labels:
          severity: critical
          team: platform-sre
        annotations:
          summary: "Agent API SLO: tier-1 burn rate ({{ $value | humanizePercentage }} error rate)"
          description: >-
            Burn rate >14x detected. Current error rate {{ $value | humanizePercentage }}.
            At this rate the 30-day error budget will be exhausted in ~2 days.
            Runbook: https://wiki/sre/agent-slo-runbook#tier1

      # Tier 2: 6x burn rate on both 30min and 6h windows
      # Exhausts 30-day budget in ~5 days
      - alert: AgentSLOBurnRateTier2
        expr: |
          slo:agent_availability:error_rate30m > (6 * 0.001)
          and
          slo:agent_availability:error_rate6h  > (6 * 0.001)
        for: 15m
        labels:
          severity: warning
          team: platform-sre
        annotations:
          summary: "Agent API SLO: tier-2 burn rate"
          description: >-
            Burn rate >6x sustained. Error budget will exhaust in ~5 days if not resolved.
            Runbook: https://wiki/sre/agent-slo-runbook#tier2
The following Python function computes the burn rate from raw time-series data and can be used in custom reporting pipelines or in testing burn rate alert logic.

from typing import List, Tuple
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

def compute_burn_rate(
    events: List[Tuple[datetime, bool]],  # (timestamp, success)
    window: timedelta,
    slo_target: float,                    # e.g. 0.999
    as_of: datetime = None,
) -> float:
    """
    Compute the error budget burn rate over the given rolling window.

    burn_rate = actual_error_rate / error_budget_rate
    error_budget_rate = 1 - slo_target

    Returns:
        burn_rate (float): 1.0 = on-track; 14.0 = budget exhausted in 1/14 of window.
    """
    if as_of is None:
        as_of = datetime.utcnow()

    window_start = as_of - window
    windowed = [(ts, ok) for ts, ok in events if window_start <= ts <= as_of]

    if not windowed:
        return 0.0

    total = len(windowed)
    errors = sum(1 for _, ok in windowed if not ok)
    actual_error_rate = errors / total
    error_budget_rate = 1 - slo_target

    if error_budget_rate == 0:
        raise ValueError("SLO target of 1.0 implies zero error budget")

    return actual_error_rate / error_budget_rate


def time_to_budget_exhaustion(
    burn_rate: float,
    remaining_budget_fraction: float,
    window: timedelta,
) -> timedelta:
    """
    Given current burn rate and remaining budget, estimate time to exhaustion.

    If burn_rate <= 0 or remaining_budget_fraction <= 0, returns infinity.
    """
    if burn_rate <= 0 or remaining_budget_fraction <= 0:
        return timedelta.max
    # At burn rate B, we consume B * (1/window) of the budget per unit time.
    # Time to exhaust = remaining_budget / (burn_rate / window_length)
    window_seconds = window.total_seconds()
    time_seconds = (remaining_budget_fraction * window_seconds) / burn_rate
    return timedelta(seconds=time_seconds)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    import random
    from datetime import timezone

    now = datetime.now(timezone.utc)
    # Simulate a 5% error rate (50x burn rate on a 99.9% SLO)
    events = [
        (now - timedelta(minutes=i), random.random() > 0.05)
        for i in range(120)  # 2 hours of 1-per-minute events
    ]

    burn = compute_burn_rate(events, window=timedelta(hours=1), slo_target=0.999)
    remaining = 0.8  # 80% of monthly budget still available
    tte = time_to_budget_exhaustion(burn, remaining, window=timedelta(days=30))

    print(f"Burn rate: {burn:.1f}x")
    print(f"Budget exhaustion in: {tte}")
    print(f"Page-worthy (>14x): {burn > 14}")
Real-world example

An agentic document processing service running at approximately 50,000 agent tasks per day has a 99.5% task completion SLO over a 30-day window. The error budget rate is 0.005, corresponding to 7,500 allowed task failures per month (250 per day at steady state).

On a Tuesday morning, a new model deployment introduces a regression in the tool-call serialization layer. Over a 45-minute window, 8% of tasks fail — a burn rate of 16x (0.08 / 0.005). This triggers both the 5-minute and 1-hour burn rate windows simultaneously, firing a tier-1 page. The on-call engineer is notified within 3 minutes of alert onset, rolls back the deployment at the 52-minute mark, and the service recovers. Total budget consumed: approximately 3,600 task failures, or about 48% of the monthly budget.

Without burn rate alerting, the team's previous threshold-based alert (fire when error rate > 5% for 10 minutes) would also have triggered, but with two key differences: it would have fired slightly later (at the 10-minute sustained threshold), and it would have provided no information about the budget impact of the incident. The burn rate alert immediately contextualizes the incident — 16x burn means the team should treat this as a critical incident, not just an elevated error rate.
Common mistakes
Using only a single window for burn rate calculation, producing either false positives (short window) or slow detection (long window).
Setting burn rate thresholds without grounding them in the SLO window length, leading to arbitrary numbers that don't map to meaningful time-to-exhaustion values.
Applying burn rate alerting to low-traffic services where a single error can appear as a 100x burn rate, causing constant false-positive pages.
Forgetting to also alert on slow (sub-1x) burns that don't page but compound over time: a 0.8x burn consumes 80% of the budget without ever triggering an alert.
Not accounting for agent task duration in the SLI: counting a failed 10-minute task the same as a failed 100ms request understates the customer impact.
Failing to test alert rules before relying on them; burn rate expressions are complex enough to have bugs that only surface during a real incident.
Best practices
Always use paired windows: each alert tier should require both a fast (sensitive) window and a slow (confirmatory) window to exceed the threshold simultaneously.
Link burn rate tier to response time expectation in runbooks: tier 1 means 'respond now', tier 2 means 'respond within 30 minutes', tier 3 means 'fix this sprint'.
Scale window lengths proportionally if using a 7-day or 24-hour SLO window instead of 30 days.
Include remaining budget in alert annotations: on-call engineers should know immediately whether they have 80% of the budget left (low urgency) or 5% (extremely high urgency).
Review alert firing history monthly: calibrate burn rate thresholds based on actual incident patterns, not just the theoretical Google SRE defaults.
For agentic AI services, define separate burn rate alerts for latency, task completion rate, and model error rate — different degradation patterns require different SLIs.
Performance and scalability

Burn rate recording rules compute over rolling windows that can span 6 hours or more of raw metric data. At high cardinality (many service instances, many label dimensions), these queries can become expensive. The standard mitigation is to pre-aggregate with recording rules at a coarser granularity (e.g., compute per-minute success counts first, then use those pre-aggregated series in the burn rate window queries) rather than computing burn rates directly from high-cardinality raw metrics.

For multi-region deployments, compute per-region burn rates as well as the aggregate. A region-local outage at 30x burn may be masked in the aggregate if other regions compensate, but it still represents a severe SLO risk for customers in that region. Include region as a label in all SLI metrics to enable both aggregate and per-region alert rules.
Tooling and ecosystem
Tool
Burn rate support
Notes
Prometheus + Alertmanager
Native via recording + alert rules
Requires manual rule writing; sloth or pyrra generate rules from SLO specs.
Datadog
Built-in SLO burn rate alerts UI
Configurable directly in the SLO dashboard; supports multi-window.
Google Cloud Monitoring
Native SLO burn rate alerts
Integrated with Cloud SLO objects; supports time-series-based and request-based SLIs.
Nobl9
Dedicated SLO platform with burn rate alerting
Multi-data-source SLIs; composites from multiple signal types.
sloth (open-source)
Generates Prometheus rules from SLO YAML
Outputs recording + alert rule files with correct multi-window burn rate expressions.

Security and reliability

The reliability of the burn rate alerting system itself is a reliability concern. If the Prometheus scrape job or Alertmanager routing pipeline goes down, burn rate alerts stop firing — and the team may not notice until a major incident is underway. Implement a 'dead man's switch' alert that fires when the alerting pipeline stops producing any output, and treat alert pipeline health as a first-class operational concern.

Alert rule changes should go through the same review process as production code changes. An incorrect burn rate threshold change could suppress legitimate pages or cause alert fatigue — either failure mode reduces the team's ability to protect the SLO. Maintain alert rules in version control and require peer review before deployment.
Key takeaways
Error budget burn rate alerts replace raw error rate thresholds with a signal proportional to SLO risk: burn rate 14x means the monthly budget exhausts in 2 days.
Multi-window alerting requires both a fast (short) window and a slow (long) window to exceed the threshold simultaneously, eliminating most false positives without sacrificing detection speed.
Three actionable tiers map burn rates to response expectations: page immediately (tier 1), page on-call (tier 2), create ticket (tier 3).

For agentic AI services, define burn rate alerts on task completion rate and quality SLIs in addition to availability — these reflect the actual customer impact of model failures more accurately than raw error rates.
Burn rate alerting only works if the underlying SLI instrumentation accurately counts all customer-visible failures; instrument at the right point in the request path.
The alerting pipeline is itself a reliability dependency — monitor it with a dead man's switch and treat alert rule changes with the same care as production code changes.
